questions.question: Explain load balancing strategies and when to use Layer 4 vs Layer 7. How do round-robin, least connections, and IP hash algorithms compare?
questions.answer: Layer 4 uses TCP/UDP for faster performance with session persistence challenges. Layer 7 inspects HTTP headers for intelligent routing (path-based, host-based). Round-robin distributes evenly, least connections handles varying request loads, IP hash maintains session affinity. AWS ALB (L7) supports path-based routing while NLB (L4) offers ultra-low latency.
questions.diagram:
graph LR User --> LB[Load Balancer] LB -->|Layer 4| S1["Server 1
IP:Port"] LB -->|Layer 7| S2["Server 2
/api"] style LB fill:#fff,stroke:#000,color:#000
questions.eli5: Imagine you're the lunch line monitor at school! When lots of kids want lunch at once, you can't let everyone rush to one lunch lady - that would make her super tired and some kids would wait forever. So you send kids to different lunch ladies so everyone gets food fast! Layer 4 is like just counting kids and sending them to the next available lunch lady - you don't care what lunch they want, just keeping the line moving. Layer 7 is like looking at each kid's lunch order first - if someone wants a sandwich, you send them to the sandwich station, if they want pizza, to the pizza line! This way, the pizza experts handle pizza orders and sandwich experts handle sandwiches. Use Layer 4 when you just need to spread people out evenly, and Layer 7 when different servers are good at different things!

questions.question: How does TCP's congestion control algorithm interact with HTTP/2's multiplexing when multiple streams compete for bandwidth?
questions.answer: TCP's congestion control operates at the transport layer and treats all HTTP/2 streams as a single connection. This creates a critical issue: when packet loss occurs on the underlying TCP connection, ALL HTTP/2 streams experience head-of-line blocking, regardless of which specific stream lost data. This means a lost packet on one stream halts progress on all other streams waiting for in-order delivery, severely degrading performance even when those other streams have no losses themselves.
questions.explanation:
## Concept Overview
TCP's congestion control algorithm manages network throughput at the transport layer, completely unaware of HTTP/2's application-layer multiplexing. This creates a fundamental architectural mismatch where TCP's connection-level control impacts all HTTP/2 streams equally.

## Implementation Details
TCP implements congestion control through algorithms like Reno, CUBIC, or BBR that maintain a congestion window (cwnd) limiting the number of unacknowledged bytes in flight. When HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple streams over this single TCP connection, all streams compete for the same bandwidth resources and are subject to identical packet loss recovery mechanisms.

## Head-of-Line Blocking: The Critical Interaction
When any packet is lost on the TCP connection, TCP must retransmit that packet before delivering any subsequent data to the application layer. Since HTTP/2 frames from all streams are interleaved within the single TCP byte stream, the receiver cannot deliver already-received frames from Stream B while waiting for the lost packet belonging to Stream A. This is head-of-line blocking at the transport layer—and it ALWAYS occurs on packet loss, making HTTP/2's multiplexing fragile on lossy networks.

## Performance Implications
This means packet loss affecting even a single HTTP/2 stream triggers congestion window reduction (via TCP's fast recovery or timeout mechanisms), slowing transmission for ALL streams. A lost packet on a low-priority stream (e.g., an image) can block a high-priority stream (e.g., critical API response), creating unpredictable latency even when streams have different priorities configured.

## Practical Impact
This is why HTTP/3 (using QUIC) moves congestion control to the stream level—each stream experiences independent head-of-line blocking, improving performance on lossy networks. For HTTP/2, this remains a fundamental limitation that developers must consider when designing APIs, often requiring multiple TCP connections (domain sharding) to work around the congestion window contention.
questions.diagram:
graph TD
    A[HTTP/2 Client] --> B[TCP Connection]
    B --> C[Congestion Control]
    C --> D[Network]
    B --> E[Stream 1]
    B --> F[Stream 2]
    B --> G[Stream 3]
    C --> H[Shared cwnd]
    H --> E
    H --> F
    H --> G

questions.question: How does QUIC solve TCP's head-of-line blocking problem in HTTP/2 multiplexing, and what are the implementation trade-offs?
questions.answer: QUIC eliminates head-of-line blocking by implementing stream-independent packet delivery over UDP, where lost packets only affect their specific stream rather than blocking the entire connection.
questions.explanation:
## Concept Overview
Head-of-line blocking occurs when a single lost packet blocks all subsequent packets, even those belonging to different streams. This significantly impacts HTTP/2 performance over TCP connections.

## Implementation Details
- **TCP Approach**: All multiplexed streams share a single TCP connection. Packet loss blocks the entire connection, affecting all streams simultaneously.
- **QUIC Approach**: Each QUIC stream operates independently. Lost packets only block their specific stream, allowing other streams to continue transmitting.
- QUIC utilizes connection IDs (rather than IP+port combinations) for connection identification and supports connection migration.

## Code Example
```bash
# TCP (HTTP/2) - blocked by single loss
Stream 1: [P

questions.question: How does QUIC solve HTTP/2's head-of-line blocking issue over TCP, and what are the implementation trade-offs?
questions.answer: QUIC runs over UDP with independent streams, eliminating TCP-level blocking while implementing reliability at the application layer.
